item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is an interesting idea, but I'm distracted by trying to imagine what this means:

Land within a 50m radius of SCP-1053 will begin to change into streets, buildings, sidewalks, and other scenery consistent with the city of [REDACTED] in 19██. Actual landmarks, street names, or neighborhoods from [REDACTED] do not exist; rather, small details (such as streetlamps, the style of buildings, etc.) are pieced together to form this new area (hereafter Ground 1053).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It took me a while to work out that the map is always at the center of Ground 1053, and thus recovering from an Emergence event requires you to enter the affected area (to get to the map and damage it enough that it stops working). "Land within a 50 m radius of SCP-1053 will begin to change" seemed to imply that Ground 1053 could emerge anywhere within that radius, and expand from there (in both internal and external dimensions).

If the radius of Ground 1053 always starts at 0 (immediately adjacent to SCP-1053) and always increases over time, then what does that initial "within a 50 m radius" refer to?

Now that I understand why Omicron-7 operates the way it does, I have an idea for an experiment: what happens if they find SCP-1053 while Ground 1053 is still small, and then try to retrieve it intact- get it back to normal space without canceling the effect first? Can it be carried outside of its own effect? Does the effect move with it? Does it stop expanding if the map isn't there?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: depends on whether or not the duct tape itself is anomalous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Which would destroy the world faster, this or 094 (the expanding portal)?

On the one hand, the city should be SCP-1053-1, and the CK-class scenario should be replaced, since CK is already defined as a dominance shift. On the other hand, though, I like the idea of it making its own randomly generated city.

Wait, maybe that's how they got the Containment Breach rooms to work! (jk)

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Let's duct tape them together, see what happens? 20 bucks on an XK

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is anyone else tempted to let loose SCP-15, SCP-1009, and SCP-1053 to see who wins?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, but I'm not exactly sure what it does. Is it supposed to be a low-cost way to make more housing? The fact that I'm upvoting in spite of my confusion speaks to my interest in the concept.

Also, I got some really heavy Invisible Cities vibes from this. Was it intentional?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: that jumped out at me, too - i was under the impression that ck was societal/cultural.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hm. I was considering it to be restructuring of the physical mass of the world, I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it! (Also, I laughed at "Bounce Houses".)

Note of personal interest: what are you considering a CK-class end of the world scenario?

Currently CK has only been used for "CK-class restructuring scenario" (a restructuring of reality which can mean a whole lot of different things). What did you have in mind for a CK-class end of the world? End of the world via restructuring of its physical mass? Would that be a subset of CK restructuring?

(Don't take this as a critique, of course. This is all just me nerding out - for some reason I really love the K-class scenarios and peoples' different takes on them.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very excellent post. Like it a lot.
And now I know what Lamed is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My favourite part is that it uses the existing area to create itself. Awesome. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Start a war. To hell with the Ethics Committee, the Foundation has done far worse in the name of SCIENCE.

Any violent and unnecessary-appearing wars from recent history come into mind? Or, at least, wars that could have had their casus belli provided by the Foundation?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it an inverse of 804?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I clarified, let me know if it's still confusing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
rather, the difference of the condition before Ground 1053 is generated is directly proportional to the amount of damage that must be dealt to SCP-1053 for Ground 1053 to disintegrate.

I'm having a difficult time figuring out what this means.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Um, declare more and more parts of the area to be unfit for living due to [falsified reason] , use foundation frontends to purchase land there and set up land uses that decrease local land value, and perhaps break local economy…
Lots of ways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Convince a bunch of 'em to move to [DATA EXPUNGED]?

item.title: Re: Inverse SCP
item.content:encoded: Nope. Jeez, people are so creative when they guess on this one!

item.title: Re: Inverse SCP
item.content:encoded: Is this the inverse to the machine that turns people into maps? I don't remember the number.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The question that I find myself asking is, what happens if the population of [REDACTED] decreases by █0,000? And what's the least morally dubious way to find out?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted for that footnote alone. Gotta keep those [REDACTED]ians down! Also, points for using letters that aren't just plain old Greek.

I can't help but think the final paragraph of the description actually belongs in the containment protocols. It's very much "This is what must be done to contain the SCP".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I rather like it… I'm just trying to figure out WHAT it's an inverse of…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a great idea… it's just really poorly written. Try to tighten it up a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"a nest of streets"

Brilliant. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I changed FALLINGSKY, but yeah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are parts of this, like the codename FALLINGSKY, Ground 1053, and other terms that aren't quite standard Foundation…whatevers. Protocols. Anyway, I like it. It works for me.

item.title: Inverse SCP
item.content:encoded: It's an inverse, as a response to one of Bright's challenges. No, it's not 184. Object Class may be subject to change…
